prompt: According to Philemon 1, who is named with Paul in the opening greeting?
choices_json: Timothy
Titus
Luke
Silas
correct_answer: Timothy
explanation: Paul opens the letter as 'Paul, a prisoner of Christ Jesus, and Timothy our brother' (Philemon 1:1).

prompt: According to Philemon 1, how does Paul describe himself?
choices_json: A prisoner of Christ Jesus
An apostle to the Gentiles
A servant of the law
A citizen of Rome
correct_answer: A prisoner of Christ Jesus
explanation: Paul identifies himself here as a prisoner of Christ Jesus (Philemon 1:1).

prompt: According to Philemon 1, to whom is the letter mainly addressed?
choices_json: Philemon
Onesimus
Apphia
Archippus
correct_answer: Philemon
explanation: The letter is addressed to Philemon, whom Paul calls beloved and a fellow worker (Philemon 1:1).

prompt: According to Philemon 1, who is called our fellow soldier?
choices_json: Archippus
Onesimus
Epaphras
Demas
correct_answer: Archippus
explanation: Paul greets Archippus and calls him our fellow soldier (Philemon 1:2).

prompt: According to Philemon 1, where was the church meeting?
choices_json: In Philemon's house
In the synagogue
In Rome
At the temple
correct_answer: In Philemon's house
explanation: Paul addresses the church in Philemon's house (Philemon 1:2).

prompt: According to Philemon 1, for whom did Paul give thanks when praying?
choices_json: Philemon
Caesar
The whole city
Only Onesimus
correct_answer: Philemon
explanation: Paul says he thanks God, always making mention of Philemon in his prayers (Philemon 1:4).

prompt: According to Philemon 1, whose hearts had been refreshed by Philemon?
choices_json: The saints
The rulers
The Romans
The travelers only
correct_answer: The saints
explanation: Paul says the hearts of the saints have been refreshed through Philemon (Philemon 1:7).

prompt: What two qualities of Philemon does Paul say he heard about in Philemon 1?
choices_json: Love and faith
Wisdom and wealth
Power and influence
Learning and courage
correct_answer: Love and faith
explanation: Paul says he heard of Philemon's love and faith toward the Lord Jesus and toward all the saints (Philemon 1:5).

prompt: Why does Paul pray regarding the fellowship of Philemon's faith?
choices_json: That it may become effective in the knowledge of every good thing in Christ
That Philemon may travel to Rome
That the church may become wealthy
That Archippus may be promoted
correct_answer: That it may become effective in the knowledge of every good thing in Christ
explanation: Paul prays that the fellowship of Philemon's faith may become effective in the knowledge of every good thing in us in Christ Jesus (Philemon 1:6).

prompt: What does Paul say he could do, though he chooses not to, in Philemon 1?
choices_json: Command what is appropriate
Leave prison immediately
Judge Onesimus publicly
Send Titus to Crete
correct_answer: Command what is appropriate
explanation: Paul says that although he has much boldness in Christ to command what is appropriate, he appeals instead for love's sake (Philemon 1:8-9).

prompt: For whom does Paul appeal in Philemon 1?
choices_json: Onesimus
Epaphras
Archippus
Apphia
correct_answer: Onesimus
explanation: Paul says, 'I appeal to you for my child, whom I have become the father of in my chains, Onesimus' (Philemon 1:10).

prompt: How had Onesimus once been described in relation to Philemon?
choices_json: Unprofitable
Faithful
Honored
Freeborn
correct_answer: Unprofitable
explanation: Paul says Onesimus once was unprofitable to Philemon, but now is profitable to both Philemon and Paul (Philemon 1:11).

prompt: How does Paul describe Onesimus when sending him back?
choices_json: My very heart
My messenger only
My fellow prisoner only
My debtor
correct_answer: My very heart
explanation: Paul says he is sending Onesimus back, 'that is, my very heart' (Philemon 1:12).

prompt: Why had Paul wanted to keep Onesimus with him?
choices_json: That he might serve Paul on Philemon's behalf in the chains of the gospel
That Onesimus could guide him to Crete
That Onesimus might write another letter
That Paul could appoint him an elder
correct_answer: That he might serve Paul on Philemon's behalf in the chains of the gospel
explanation: Paul says he wished to keep Onesimus so that he might serve him on Philemon's behalf in the bonds of the gospel (Philemon 1:13).

prompt: Why does Paul appeal rather than command in Philemon 1?
choices_json: He wants Philemon's response to arise from love and willingness rather than pressure
He lacks any authority to speak
He is unsure Onesimus changed
He wants to avoid mentioning the gospel
correct_answer: He wants Philemon's response to arise from love and willingness rather than pressure
explanation: Paul says that for love's sake he rather appeals, and he wants Philemon's goodness not to be by compulsion but of free will (Philemon 1:9, 14).

prompt: What does the change in Onesimus's description suggest in Philemon 1?
choices_json: The gospel has transformed someone once useless into someone now truly useful
Paul is making a joke without deeper meaning
Philemon had misjudged Onesimus's work ethic only
Onesimus became profitable through Roman law
correct_answer: The gospel has transformed someone once useless into someone now truly useful
explanation: Paul deliberately contrasts Onesimus as formerly unprofitable and now profitable, highlighting gospel change (Philemon 1:11).

prompt: How does Philemon 1 redefine Onesimus's relationship to Philemon?
choices_json: No longer merely as a servant, but as a beloved brother
As a stranger who should be avoided
As a public example of shame
As a hired laborer only
correct_answer: No longer merely as a servant, but as a beloved brother
explanation: Paul asks Philemon to receive Onesimus no longer as a servant only, but more than a servant, a beloved brother (Philemon 1:16).

prompt: Why does Paul mention that Onesimus may have departed for a while?
choices_json: He interprets the separation in light of God's providence and lasting restoration
He is excusing sin as unimportant
He is claiming prophetic certainty about every detail
He wants Philemon to forget the past entirely
correct_answer: He interprets the separation in light of God's providence and lasting restoration
explanation: Paul says perhaps Onesimus was separated for a while so Philemon might have him back forever, suggesting a providential reading of events (Philemon 1:15).

prompt: What does Paul mean by asking Philemon to receive Onesimus as he would receive Paul?
choices_json: Philemon should extend to Onesimus the honor and welcome due to gospel fellowship
Onesimus is replacing Paul as an apostle
Philemon should send Onesimus back immediately to prison
Paul wants Onesimus to take his place in the church leadership
correct_answer: Philemon should extend to Onesimus the honor and welcome due to gospel fellowship
explanation: Paul says if Philemon counts him a partner, he should receive Onesimus as he would receive Paul (Philemon 1:17).

prompt: Why does Paul offer to repay any wrong or debt in Philemon 1?
choices_json: He is removing obstacles to reconciliation and bearing the cost personally
He doubts Philemon's honesty
He wants to purchase Onesimus as property
He is collecting debts for the church
correct_answer: He is removing obstacles to reconciliation and bearing the cost personally
explanation: Paul tells Philemon to charge any wrong or debt to his account and says he will repay it (Philemon 1:18-19).

prompt: What does Paul's confidence in Philemon's obedience show?
choices_json: Paul expects gospel-shaped love to produce even more generosity than he requests
Paul fears Philemon will refuse outright
Paul is forcing obedience through threat alone
Paul no longer trusts Philemon at all
correct_answer: Paul expects gospel-shaped love to produce even more generosity than he requests
explanation: Paul writes with confidence in Philemon's obedience, knowing he will do even beyond what Paul says (Philemon 1:21).

prompt: How does Philemon 1 blend personal affection with apostolic authority?
choices_json: Paul has authority to command, yet he uses affection and partnership to seek voluntary gospel obedience
Paul abandons all authority in the letter
Authority appears only in the greeting and nowhere else
Affection replaces any moral claim on Philemon
correct_answer: Paul has authority to command, yet he uses affection and partnership to seek voluntary gospel obedience
explanation: Paul notes he could command what is fitting, but instead appeals for love's sake and for willing goodness (Philemon 1:8-9, 14).

prompt: What role does partnership play in Paul's appeal in Philemon 1?
choices_json: Paul uses shared fellowship in Christ as the basis for how Philemon should receive Onesimus
Partnership is mentioned only as a travel arrangement
Paul uses partnership to avoid speaking about sin
Partnership here refers only to financial support
correct_answer: Paul uses shared fellowship in Christ as the basis for how Philemon should receive Onesimus
explanation: Paul says if Philemon counts him a partner, he should receive Onesimus as he would receive Paul (Philemon 1:17).

prompt: How does the letter transform a social relationship without directly staging a public revolution?
choices_json: It places master and servant inside brotherhood in Christ, changing the relationship at its deepest level
It ignores social realities completely
It commands immediate political revolt in Crete
It treats Onesimus only as a legal problem
correct_answer: It places master and servant inside brotherhood in Christ, changing the relationship at its deepest level
explanation: Paul asks Philemon to receive Onesimus no longer merely as a servant but as a beloved brother, especially in the Lord (Philemon 1:16).

prompt: Why is Paul's self-offering to pay Onesimus's debt so significant?
choices_json: It mirrors the gospel pattern of costly reconciliation by taking another's liability upon himself
It proves Paul was financially wealthy
It excuses Onesimus from any moral responsibility
It shows the dispute was only about money
correct_answer: It mirrors the gospel pattern of costly reconciliation by taking another's liability upon himself
explanation: Paul's offer to repay what Onesimus owes reinforces his effort to reconcile the two and reflects a substitutionary pattern within the appeal (Philemon 1:18-19).

prompt: How does Philemon 1 connect hospitality, prayer, and reconciliation?
choices_json: Paul expects the same grace seen in Philemon's past refreshment of saints to govern his reception of Onesimus and his welcome of Paul
These themes are unrelated in the letter
Prayer replaces practical action in Paul's argument
Hospitality is mentioned only for travel convenience
correct_answer: Paul expects the same grace seen in Philemon's past refreshment of saints to govern his reception of Onesimus and his welcome of Paul
explanation: Paul praises Philemon for refreshing the saints, asks him to refresh Paul, and then requests lodging, linking his established character to the present appeal (Philemon 1:7, 20, 22).

prompt: What does the providential 'perhaps' of Philemon 1 accomplish rhetorically?
choices_json: It invites Philemon to see God's possible purpose in painful events without speaking presumptuously
It weakens Paul's appeal into uncertainty only
It denies that Onesimus did anything wrong
It removes the need for Philemon's response
correct_answer: It invites Philemon to see God's possible purpose in painful events without speaking presumptuously
explanation: Paul says 'perhaps' he was separated for a while so that Philemon might have him back forever, suggesting providence with pastoral modesty (Philemon 1:15).

prompt: How does the letter use honor and shame dynamics for a redemptive purpose?
choices_json: Paul publicly honors Philemon's love and then calls him to act consistently with that identity toward Onesimus
Paul shames Philemon harshly before the churches
Honor is irrelevant to the letter's persuasion
Paul appeals only to Roman law
correct_answer: Paul publicly honors Philemon's love and then calls him to act consistently with that identity toward Onesimus
explanation: Paul begins by praising Philemon's love, faith, and refreshment of the saints, then builds his appeal on that known character and partnership (Philemon 1:5-7, 17, 20-21).

prompt: How does Philemon 1 demonstrate the gospel's power to reorder human relationships?
choices_json: It reframes a damaged master-servant relationship through brotherhood, reconciliation, and voluntary love in Christ
It keeps the gospel strictly private and inward
It treats social bonds as unchanged by conversion
It focuses only on debt collection
correct_answer: It reframes a damaged master-servant relationship through brotherhood, reconciliation, and voluntary love in Christ
explanation: Paul's whole appeal asks Philemon to receive Onesimus not merely by old social categories but as a beloved brother and partner in the Lord (Philemon 1:15-17).

prompt: Why is Paul's choice of appeal over command theologically rich?
choices_json: It shows that gospel obedience reaches its fullest form when love freely embraces what grace requires
It means apostolic authority is unimportant
It proves Paul is uncertain about right and wrong
It suggests commands have no place in Christian life
correct_answer: It shows that gospel obedience reaches its fullest form when love freely embraces what grace requires
explanation: Paul wants Philemon's goodness not to be as of necessity but willingly, revealing a vision of transformed, voluntary obedience (Philemon 1:8-9, 14).

prompt: How does Paul's mediation in Philemon echo broader gospel patterns without explicitly stating them?
choices_json: Paul stands between offender and offended, identifying with both and offering to bear the debt to achieve reconciliation
Paul remains neutral and detached from the conflict
He resolves the matter only through legal procedure
He minimizes wrongdoing so reconciliation costs nothing
correct_answer: Paul stands between offender and offended, identifying with both and offering to bear the debt to achieve reconciliation
explanation: Paul calls Onesimus his child and heart, yet appeals to Philemon as partner and offers to repay the debt himself, functioning as a reconciler (Philemon 1:10, 12, 17-19).

prompt: What does the letter imply about Christian identity and social status?
choices_json: Union in Christ relativizes old status distinctions by introducing a deeper identity as beloved brotherhood
Social status is the final truth about a person
Only spiritual status matters and embodied relationships do not
Paul denies any social obligations remain after conversion
correct_answer: Union in Christ relativizes old status distinctions by introducing a deeper identity as beloved brotherhood
explanation: Paul's request that Onesimus be received as more than a servant, as a beloved brother in the Lord, makes Christian identity decisive (Philemon 1:16).

prompt: Why is refreshment a key theme in Philemon 1?
choices_json: The letter moves from Philemon's past refreshment of the saints to Paul's hope that Philemon will refresh him by reconciling with Onesimus
Refreshment refers only to food and lodging
It is a minor courtesy with no rhetorical role
Paul uses it to avoid discussing Onesimus directly
correct_answer: The letter moves from Philemon's past refreshment of the saints to Paul's hope that Philemon will refresh him by reconciling with Onesimus
explanation: Paul praises Philemon because the saints' hearts were refreshed through him and later says, 'refresh my heart in the Lord,' tying refreshment to the requested act of grace (Philemon 1:7, 20).

prompt: How does Philemon 1 show that theology can be communicated through personal correspondence rather than abstract treatise?
choices_json: A short, situational letter embodies doctrines of grace, fellowship, reconciliation, providence, and transformed identity through lived relationships
The letter contains no real theology because it is personal
Only formal doctrinal essays can express theology
Paul avoids theological implications by staying practical
correct_answer: A short, situational letter embodies doctrines of grace, fellowship, reconciliation, providence, and transformed identity through lived relationships
explanation: The letter's appeal over Onesimus draws together themes of grace, love, partnership, providence, debt-bearing, and brotherhood in Christ (Philemon 1:6, 14-19).

prompt: What overarching pastoral vision governs Philemon 1?
choices_json: The gospel creates a community where authority, affection, and costly reconciliation work together to restore broken relationships
The main goal is simply to settle a private dispute quietly
Paul is primarily interested in recovering lost property
The letter minimizes the role of the church in personal conflict
correct_answer: The gospel creates a community where authority, affection, and costly reconciliation work together to restore broken relationships
explanation: Paul's appeal to Philemon joins church fellowship, apostolic concern, personal love, and willingness to bear cost so reconciliation can happen in the Lord (Philemon 1:1-22).
